URL: https://cameroncounts.wordpress.com/2023/10/01/challenges-in-combinatorial-design-theory/

SUMMARY:
I jotted down three problems as a challenge for magicians wielding the new powerful methods. The first, of course, is the existence of projective planes.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://cameroncounts.wordpress.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/11/1to19.jpg)

## [Challenges in combinatorial design theory](https://cameroncounts.wordpress.com/2023/10/01/challenges-in-combinatorial-design-theory/)

Since WordPress changed their editor and it is no longer possible to write posts in HTML, I have to find a new solution to posting mathematics.

What I have done this time is to put it on my web page on GitHub (<https://cameroncounts.github.io/web/>) and link it from here.

On Thursday last week, Peter Keevash came to talk to the Pure Maths Colloquium in St Andrews about some of his really astonishing breakthroughs on the asymptotic existence and enumeration of combinatorial designs, including t-designs, Latin squares, Sudoku squares, and many others. But there are some problems he cannot solve, including Ryser’s and Brualdi’s conjectures on Latin squares. So I jotted down three problems as a challenge for magicians wielding the new powerful methods. The first, of course, is the existence of projective planes, which he mentioned in his talk. The second is a generalisation of the existence of resolvable designs; his methods may well solve this already. The third is a perhaps lesser known problem due to Mohammed Aljohani, John Bamberg and me, to which Steiner sytems are the proposed answer rather than the subject of the problem.

## About Peter Cameron

### 7 Responses to *Challenges in combinatorial design theory*

![Jon Awbrey's avatar](https://2.gravatar.com/avatar/587a0ac279c08c47415d07013ee06ec147e8391483bd99f8e616c389f6e37a26?s=40&d=identicon&r=G)

Do you not still have the Classic Editor? I use both HTML and LaTeX in that.

![Sean Eberhard's avatar](https://0.gravatar.com/avatar/38e73cc0b69b2b61890802a6156a0859feb4e3af588b42a8b4b6a9a0591d5e01?s=40&d=identicon&r=G)

1. As far as I know you can still write posts in HTML on wordpress. From the page on which you write your post, you click the three dots in the top-right corner, and then find “code editor”.

2. Is there a reference for John Thompson’s rival conjecture about orders of projective planes? It sounds deliberately provocative, the point being we know approximately nothing about projective planes of orders as large as 60, and from that point of view one could argue it is just as reasonable to conjecture they are all prime powers as they are all powers of orders of simple groups.

![Peter Cameron's avatar](https://0.gravatar.com/avatar/ff9c2514f1775978815e561990deec4aca23c28755cb97f3e44d4ea401f21e0b?s=40&d=identicon&r=G)

On 2, I don’t know a reference. He talked about this at a conference in Oxford (a birthday meeting for Dan Segal and Aner Shalev) in 2018 (if my memory is correct). He was quite frail, and had to give his talk sitting down, so not everything was clear to me, but this conjecture was so striking I remembered it.  
Thompson thought deeply about projective planes (his paper with Jessie MacWilliams and Neil Sloane was one of the preliminaries to the nonexistence proof for order 10), and even talked about the topic in a BCC lecture quite some time ago, but this was the only time I heard him make this conjecture.

![Dima's avatar](https://2.gravatar.com/avatar/816a256f48bb0577db934daa098dcb304a223b3e7f7d54d84feafbe760381f9d?s=40&d=identicon&r=G)

You can move the whole proceedings to your GitHub pages, by enabling comments there, e.g. by adding free <https://utteranc.es/> plugin

![Peter Cameron's avatar](https://0.gravatar.com/avatar/ff9c2514f1775978815e561990deec4aca23c28755cb97f3e44d4ea401f21e0b?s=40&d=identicon&r=G)

Thanks all for your comments about HTML in WordPress. When they changed the default editor, I proceeded in my usual way, pasting in a pre-polished HTML file, and the result was gibberish, so I didn’t look further. I might experiment before I give up on WordPress.

![Mike Smith's avatar](https://2.gravatar.com/avatar/5b6ded05f41e3228166b5383d246788215cfb9d2b5c55dbae9af84654991f7d1?s=40&d=identicon&r=G)

Given Eberhard’s question and your response, Peter, I’m reminded of the following question: Does there exist a finite projective plane with no nontrivial automorphisms?  
What about infinite planes? Doing a quick Google search didn’t lead to anything directly related to this question other than some simple (and good?) upper bounds on IAut(P)I in terms of the order n. Another nice question I did run across here is are there groups which can’t be subgroups of Aut(P) for any finite n? Large alternating groups are proposed as examples here.

![Peter Cameron's avatar](https://0.gravatar.com/avatar/ff9c2514f1775978815e561990deec4aca23c28755cb97f3e44d4ea401f21e0b?s=40&d=identicon&r=G)

Dear Mike,

Good questions.

I am almost certain that there are infinite planes without nontrivial automorphisms. The examples I have in mind are free planes, where you start with a finite “partial plane” and then alternately add a line through each pair of non-collnear points and a point on each pair of non-concurrent lines. There are results telling you that, under suitable hypotheses, you can identify the starting configuration. So start with something with trivial automorphism group, and you have it.

We are very far from finding a finite projective plane with no non-trivial automorphisms. (Historical note: it was known that a putative plane of order 10 would be automorphism-free before the proof that no such plane exists.) On the subject of groups which can’t act on planes, Christoph Hering did a lot of work on this, and showed that for most cases in the Lenz–Barlotti classification there are strong restrictions on the groups that can arise. But the case he says nothing about is where there are central collineations with a common centre and axis. So, for example, we don’t know why there can’t be a plane having an elation group which is a large alternating group with fixed centre and axis.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent breakthroughs in combinatorial designs enhance coding theory applications, fostering innovative error-correcting codes crucial for robust communication systems, as noted in research findings.

The fusion of combinatorial designs with emerging technologies like quantum computing and artificial intelligence promises novel algorithms and improved efficiency in data transmission and error correction.

Utilizing advanced combinatorial structures can lead to superior error-correcting codes, significantly reducing data loss and enhancing reliability in critical applications such as telecommunications and data storage.
